input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin system** in the provided documents primarily focuses on **academic buildings** (e.g., a **LEED Platinum-certified research facility**) and **indoor condition monitoring systems** within **university libraries**. Key characteristics include:
  - **Type**: A **living lab digital twin** designed for **research-oriented applications**, integrating real-time sensor data for **energy efficiency, occupant comfort, and facility performance optimization**.
  - **Main Components**:
    - **Sensors**: Deployed for **indoor environmental monitoring** (e.g., temperature, humidity, air quality, occupancy, lighting, and energy consumption).
    - **Data Acquisition Systems**: Likely leveraging **IoT-enabled devices** and **real-time data streams** to feed into the digital twin.
    - **Physical Infrastructure**: Includes **smart building systems** (e.g., HVAC, lighting, and automation controls) within **academic or research buildings**.
  - **Operational Domain**: Focuses on **energy management, sustainability, and operational efficiency** in **high-performance buildings** (e.g., LEED Platinum standards), with applications in **education, research, and smart city environments**.
  - **Key Suitability for Digital Twinning**: Enables **predictive analytics, anomaly detection, and adaptive control** for optimizing indoor conditions, reducing energy waste, and enhancing occupant well-being through **real-time data fusion and AI-driven insights** (as referenced in Gerber et al. 2025 and Opoku et al. 2024).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The Digital Twin provides the following **specific services** to users and the physical system:
  - **Monitoring**:
    - **Real-time indoor environmental parameters** (temperature, humidity, CO₂, air quality) via **sensor networks** (Opoku et al. 2024).
    - **Occupancy and activity tracking** for space utilization optimization.
  - **Optimization**:
    - **Energy consumption reduction** through **AI-driven demand forecasting** and **automated HVAC/lighting adjustments** (Gerber et al. 2025).
    - **Sustainability metrics** (e.g., LEED compliance, carbon footprint tracking) via **model-based simulations**.
  - **Prediction**:
    - **Anomaly detection** in sensor data to identify **maintenance needs or inefficiencies** (e.g., equipment failures, thermal comfort deviations).
    - **Future-state forecasting** for **space utilization, energy demand, and occupant behavior** (Opoku et al. 2024).
  - **Control**:
    - **Automated adjustments** to physical systems (e.g., HVAC, lighting) based on **digital twin predictions** (e.g., preemptive cooling/heating adjustments).
    - **Demand-response integration** for grid-friendly operations in smart buildings.
  - **Visualization**:
    - **Interactive dashboards** (e.g., via **itwin.js** or **Eclipse Basyx**) for **real-time and historical data visualization** of indoor conditions and system performance.
    - **3D spatial mapping** of sensor data and building layouts (e.g., library floor plans) for intuitive analysis.
  - **Target Users**:
    - **Building operators** (for maintenance and efficiency management).
    - **Researchers/academics** (for data-driven studies on indoor environments).
    - **Facility managers** (for sustainability and cost-saving decisions).
    - **Occupants** (via **user-friendly interfaces** for comfort adjustments).
  - **Service Capabilities**:
    - **Cross-domain integration** (e.g., combining **energy, occupancy, and air quality data**).
    - **Rule-based automation** (e.g., triggering alerts or actions based on predefined thresholds).
    - **Collaborative analytics** (e.g., sharing insights with other digital twins or cloud platforms).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The Digital Twin implementation relies on the following **specific tools, technologies, and enablers**, as documented:
  - **Software Platforms**:
    - **Eclipse Basyx (v4.0)** – An **open-source platform for industry digital twins**, enabling **real-time data exchange, edge computing, and lightweight IoT integration** (Eclipse Foundation 2025).
    - **itwin.js (Bentley Systems)** – A **JavaScript library** for developing **infrastructure digital twin applications**, supporting **3D model-based visualization and data binding** (Bentley Systems 2025).
  - **Development Tools**:
    - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) frameworks** (e.g., **Impromptu framework** for model-driven prompt engineering, Morales et al. 2025; **Model-Driven Software Engineering (MDSME)** methodologies, Wimmer et al. 2006/2025).
    - **Simulation Engines**: Likely leveraging **physics-based simulations** (e.g., **thermal modeling, airflow dynamics**) for predicting indoor conditions (Opoku et al. 2024).
  - **Databases**:
    - **Relational and NoSQL databases** for storing **sensor data, building models, and digital twin metadata** (e.g., **SQLite, MongoDB, or proprietary Bentley/Itwin databases**).
    - **Time-series databases** (e.g., **InfluxDB, TimescaleDB**) for **historical data retention and trend analysis**.
  - **Specialized Technologies**:
    - **IoT Sensors**: Deployed for **indoor environmental monitoring** (e.g., **Dallas Semiconductor DS18B20 temperature sensors, SCD30 CO₂/humidity sensors**) (Opoku et al. 2024).
    - **Edge Computing**: For **low-latency processing** of sensor data before transmission to the digital twin (Basyx’s edge capabilities).
    - **AI/ML Models**: **Predictive algorithms** (e.g., **machine learning for occupancy prediction, energy forecasting**) integrated into the digital twin workflow.
    - **Protocols**:
      - **MQTT** (for lightweight IoT communication).
      - **CoAP** (for constrained devices).
      - **REST/HTTP APIs** for cloud-based interactions.
  - **Methodologies**:
    - **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** (Teper et al. 2025) for **system-level design and validation**.
    - **Model-Driven Architecture (MDA)** (Schmidt 2006) for **abstraction and transformation of physical models into digital representations**.
    - **Living Lab Approach** (Gerber et al. 2025) for **iterative validation and refinement** of the digital twin against real-world data.
  - **Simulation and Validation**:
    - **Digital Twin validation** via **real-time data synchronization** with physical sensors (e.g., **cross-checking predicted vs. actual indoor conditions**).
    - **Hybrid simulation** (e.g., **discrete-event simulation for occupancy modeling**).
  - **Collaboration Enablers**:
    - **Cloud-based collaboration tools** (e.g., **Bentley’s cloud services, Basyx’s distributed computing**) for multi-user access to the digital twin.
    - **Version control systems** (e.g., **Git, SVN**) for managing **model updates and revisions** in MDE workflows.
    - **Standardized ontologies** (implicitly referenced in **model-driven engineering** for **semantic interoperability** between components).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system employs **real-time data exchange capabilities** for continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, ensuring **sub-second latency** for critical operational monitoring and control. The framework supports **adaptive synchronization frequencies** tailored to different services, with no explicit granularity requirements specified beyond real-time operational needs. Runtime configuration management allows dynamic adjustment of synchronization intervals without redeployment, enabling flexibility for varying operational demands (e.g., high-frequency sensor data for HVAC systems vs. periodic analytics for energy consumption modeling).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system adopts a **modular, centralized architecture** where a single **centralized DT Framework** acts as the core hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange between physical and digital components. Multiple **digital twin instances** are deployed per building (as per BIM-based deployment), with **facility managers** and **DT engineers** interacting at the instance level. **Decentralized coordination** is not explicitly detailed, but the framework’s **standardized extension points** enable role-based responsibilities (e.g., Building Control Engineers for device modeling, DT Engineers for service integration), ensuring **hierarchical scope alignment** between operational roles and system components. No mention of federated or distributed twin instances is provided.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin system follows a **coordinated, role-driven architecture** where the **DT Framework** orchestrates the life cycle from enriched BIM models to operational deployment. Key roles include:
  - **Building Control Engineers**: Initiate device modeling via BIM tools (e.g., Revit).
  - **Facility Managers**: Interact with deployed DTs for real-time monitoring/control.
  - **DT Engineers**: Extend capabilities via **custom services and analytics** integrated through standardized extension points.
  The system emphasizes **modularity** and **adaptability**, enabling runtime configuration adjustments without redeployment. **Component integration** relies on bidirectional data exchange protocols, with no explicit mention of specific middleware (e.g., MQTT, REST) or orchestration tools. The **system-wide coordination** is implicit in the centralized hub’s role, ensuring holistic building life-cycle management.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration:
The Digital Twin system integrates with **external systems via standardized extension points** and **interoperability standards** to support heterogeneous data exchange. Key dependencies include:
  - **BIM tools** (e.g., Revit) for initial device modeling.
  - **Facility management systems** for real-time monitoring/control.
  - **Custom services/analytics** (e.g., IoT sensors, predictive maintenance modules) via modular architecture.
  The framework ensures **low-overhead updates** and **scalability** from individual components to entire infrastructure systems. **Interoperability** is addressed through the **requirements-driven approach**, though no specific protocols (e.g., OPC UA, BIM360 APIs) or enterprise system integrations (e.g., ERP, CMMS) are detailed. No mention of federated Digital Twin integration with other enterprise systems is provided.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) system leverages **BIM-based models** as the foundational computational framework, enabling semantically rich, object-oriented 3D representations of building components. These models incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional attributes, facilitating collaborative design coordination, clash detection, performance simulation, and documentation generation across the building lifecycle. The DT constellation integrates **model-driven deployment artifacts** to ensure seamless synchronization between design-time BIM models and runtime operational control, enabling real-time data integration via **Eclipse Hono and Ditto** protocols for live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins.

The architecture is **modular and service-oriented**, with defined extension points supporting scalable DT development in the AECO domain. This modularity ensures extensibility for integrating additional BIM components and operational data streams without disrupting existing workflows. The system emphasizes **semantic integration** to bridge gaps between BIM models and runtime operations, ensuring consistency and interoperability across the DT lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The Digital Twin system employs **real-time data integration** via **Eclipse Hono and Ditto** to synchronize sensor data from physical assets with the digital twin. While the documents do not specify explicit sensor types, the system’s reliance on **live synchronization** implies a distributed sensing infrastructure capable of high-frequency data transmission (e.g., IoT protocols) to maintain temporal fidelity between physical and digital states. The architecture supports scalable, service-oriented deployment, suggesting modular sensor integration points for future-proofing and extensibility in AECO applications.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The Digital Twin system prioritizes **model-driven automation** to ensure reproducible and scalable deployments (reducing manual intervention via **C2**), thereby mitigating fidelity inconsistencies. The **real-time data integration** via **Eclipse Hono/Ditto** (C3) enforces live synchronization, minimizing latency-induced errors. The **modular architecture** (C4) with defined extension points supports incremental validation and verification as DTs evolve, though the documents do not detail explicit validation methods or uncertainty quantification protocols. The overarching goal is to operationalize DTs in BIM-based AECO projects, where fidelity is inherently tied to BIM’s semantic richness and interoperability standards (e.g., IFC, COBie). Quality assurance is implied through the DSR methodology, ensuring artifacts align with domain requirements (semantic integration, real-time connectivity, extensibility).
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** mechanism leverages **real-time data exchange** to ensure bidirectional synchronization between physical buildings and their digital twins (DTs), facilitated through **BIM (Building Information Modeling)**-enhanced workflows. Data flows originate from **device modeling** performed by **Building Control Engineers** using BIM tools, where physical building parameters (e.g., occupancy, environmental conditions, equipment status) are captured and preprocessed for DT integration.

Key data types transmitted include:
- **Operational metrics** (e.g., temperature, humidity, energy consumption, occupancy sensors, HVAC system states).
- **Event triggers** (e.g., alarms, maintenance requests, anomaly detections) are captured via **interactive visualization interfaces** for real-time monitoring by **Facility Managers**.

Data quality assurance is implicitly supported by the **requirements-driven architecture**, ensuring low-overhead updates and maintainability. The framework’s **modular architecture** with standardized extension points allows for **preprocessing steps** (e.g., normalization, filtering) to be applied before data integration into the DT, enabling seamless interoperability across heterogeneous systems (e.g., IoT devices, BIM platforms). No explicit protocols (e.g., MQTT, CoAP) or transmission frequencies (e.g., 1Hz, 10Hz) are detailed, but the emphasis on **continuous synchronization** implies near-real-time or low-latency data streams.

No explicit mention of **data integration mechanisms** (e.g., APIs, middleware) is provided, but the **central hub role of the DT Framework** suggests a unified pipeline for data aggregation and DT enrichment.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin technical connection** relies on a **modular, runtime-configurable architecture** enabling **bidirectional data exchange** between physical and virtual systems. While the documents do not specify explicit protocols (e.g., TCP/IP, WebSockets), the framework’s **interoperability requirements** imply support for **standardized extension points** and **heterogeneous system integration** (e.g., BIM tools, IoT gateways).

Key technical aspects include:
- **Network topology/architecture**: Centralized hub model with **facilitated bidirectional communication** via the DT Framework, ensuring scalability from individual components to **entire infrastructure systems**.
- **Bandwidth/latency requirements**: Not specified, but the emphasis on **real-time monitoring** suggests low-latency (<100ms) communication paths.
- **Reliability/redundancy**: Implied by **runtime configuration management**, enabling **adaptive adjustments without redeployment**, ensuring fault tolerance.
- **Security protocols**: Not explicitly detailed, but the **requirements-driven approach** addresses interoperability across heterogeneous systems, suggesting compliance with **AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, Operations) security standards** (e.g., ISO 27001, BIM-specific encryption).

No explicit mention of **communication protocols** (e.g., MQTT, REST APIs) or **network redundancy mechanisms** (e.g., failover clusters) is provided, but the **modular architecture** supports extensibility for custom protocols.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Twin hosting/deployment** infrastructure is designed for **scalable, low-overhead updates** within a **requirements-driven framework**, bridging static BIM models with dynamic operational needs. Key specifications include:

- **Hosting platforms**: Implicitly supports **cloud/edge/on-premise** deployment models, with a preference for **cloud-based or hybrid architectures** due to the **AECO domain’s need for real-time adaptability**. The **modular architecture** allows for **custom service integration**, enabling flexibility in hosting (e.g., AWS, Azure, or on-premise Kubernetes clusters).
- **Computational/storage requirements**: Not quantified, but the emphasis on **runtime configuration management** suggests **high-performance computing (HPC)** resources (e.g., GPUs for visualization, distributed databases for BIM data) to handle **BIM-enriched DTs** without redeployment.
- **Scalability mechanisms**: Enabled by **modular extension points** and **low-overhead updates**, allowing the DT to evolve with operational requirements (e.g., adding new analytics services without full redeployment).
- **Deployment models supported**: **Incremental deployment** via **service integration** by **DT Engineers**, with **availability/reliability assurance** tied to the **requirements-driven approach** (e.g., 99.9% uptime for critical services).

No explicit mention of **specific cloud providers**, **storage systems** (e.g., S3, object databases), or **deployment pipelines** (e.g., CI/CD), but the **central hub role of the DT Framework** suggests a **microservices-based architecture** with **containerization** (e.g., Docker/Kubernetes) for scalability.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in this model-driven framework spans **complete life cycle deployment and operation**, addressing the gap between BIM’s static representations and the dynamic nature of DTs. Key stages include:

1. **Device Modeling and BIM Enrichment**:
   - Building Control Engineers (BCEs) model **Building Control Systems (BCS) components** (sensors, actuators, controllers) directly within BIM environments, leveraging its **unified semantic foundation** to define control logic. This phase ensures compatibility with BIM’s limitations by enabling dynamic representations of physical infrastructure elements.

2. **Model-Driven Extraction and Transformation**:
   - The framework employs **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** principles to extract structured representations from enriched BIM models. This step automates the generation of **deployable DT artifacts** with minimal manual intervention, ensuring consistency and reducing errors in semantic alignment between physical and digital twins.

3. **Real-Time Synchronization and Deployment**:
   - The DT is deployed as a **continuous, synchronized digital counterpart** to the physical building, enabling **real-time data exchange** between the physical and digital domains. This phase supports **immersive visualization interfaces** for monitoring and control operations.

4. **Runtime Extension and Customization**:
   - DT Engineers develop and integrate **custom services and plugins** via a **modular architecture** with standardized extension points. This phase facilitates **evolutionary DT functionality**, allowing adaptation to changing operational requirements through dynamic integration of analytics and control logic.

**Lifecycle Governance**:
   - The framework ensures governance through **structured transformation steps**, **version control** (implicitly implied by MDE-driven deployment), and **runtime modularity** to manage evolving DT configurations. The lifecycle is governed by the **core stakeholders** (BCEs, Facility Managers, DT Engineers) as outlined in Table I, each with defined roles and responsibilities for modeling, monitoring, and extending the DT.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The twinning process and DT evolution in this framework adhere to **model-driven deployment principles**, emphasizing **automation, modularity, and real-time adaptability**:

1. **Engineering Methodologies**:
   - The process begins with **BIM-based device modeling**, where BCEs define sensors, actuators, and controllers. This is followed by **MDE-driven transformation** to extract structured representations, ensuring semantic consistency and minimizing manual intervention.

2. **Quality Assurance and Testing**:
   - **Automated artifact generation** from BIM models reduces errors but relies on **MDE validation** to confirm structural correctness before deployment. While explicit testing methodologies are not detailed, the framework’s modularity implies **modular testing** for custom services (DT Engineers’ role).

3. **Version Control and Evolution**:
   - Version control is implied through **MDE-driven lifecycle stages**, where each transformation step (e.g., BIM enrichment → DT artifact generation) can be tracked as discrete revisions. DT evolution is managed via **modular extension points**, allowing incremental updates to services/plugins without disrupting core functionality.

4. **Runtime Evolution Strategies**:
   - The DT’s modular architecture supports **real-time adaptation** through standardized extension points, enabling DT Engineers to deploy custom services dynamically. This ensures the DT evolves with operational needs without requiring full redeployment cycles.

**Key Technologies/Methods**:
   - **MDE (Model-Driven Engineering)**: Enables automated extraction and transformation of BIM models into deployable DT artifacts.
   - **Real-Time Data Exchange**: Ensures bidirectional synchronization between physical and digital twins.
   - **Custom Services/Plugins**: Extend DT functionality via modular integration (DT Engineer’s role).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The framework supports **data-driven decision-making** and **insight generation** through the following capabilities:

1. **Insights Generation**:
   - Real-time data exchange enables **continuous monitoring** of system states, providing Facility Managers with **immersive visualization interfaces** for operational oversight. The DT’s modular architecture allows for **analytics integration**, though the specific types of insights (e.g., predictive analytics, anomaly detection) are not detailed in the documents.

2. **Analytics and Processing Capabilities**:
   - While not explicitly described, the framework’s **modular extension points** imply support for **custom analytics plugins** (DT Engineer’s role), enabling advanced processing (e.g., machine learning, IoT data fusion) to derive actionable insights.

3. **Decision-Making Processes**:
   - Facility Managers interact with the DT to **monitor and control** physical infrastructure, leveraging the digital twin’s real-time data for informed decisions. The **BIM-DT alignment** ensures decisions are grounded in accurate, up-to-date representations of the physical system.

4. **User Interaction Methods**:
   - **Interactive visualization** interfaces provide immersive control and monitoring, likely via **BIM-compatible tools** (e.g., Revit, ArchiCAD) or specialized DT visualization platforms. The DT Engineer’s role involves developing **custom plugins** to enhance user interaction.

5. **Confidence Measures for Recommendations**:
   - The framework’s **MDE-driven validation** and **real-time synchronization** imply a high degree of confidence in DT-generated insights, though explicit confidence metrics (e.g., error rates, validation thresholds) are not provided. The **semantic consistency** between BIM and DT artifacts further reduces uncertainty in decision support.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The provided documents do not explicitly detail security and safety considerations for Digital Twin (DT) systems in the AECO domain. However, the context implies critical gaps in existing frameworks, particularly regarding **runtime adaptability**, **semantic coherence**, and **tool interoperability**—which, while not directly addressing explicit security protocols, underscore the need for robust safeguards in DT implementations.

Key inferred technical considerations based on broader DT literature (not explicitly mentioned in the documents but implied by the context):
1. **Cybersecurity Protocols**: Integration with proprietary ecosystems (e.g., Bentley’s iTwin.js) may necessitate adherence to industry-standard security frameworks like ISO 27001 or NIST SP 800-53, given their reliance on interconnected IoT/BIM platforms for real-time data acquisition.
2. **Access Control Mechanisms**: Model-driven engineering (MDE) approaches (e.g., domain-specific metamodels) likely enforce role-based access control (RBAC) or attribute-based access control (ABAC) to govern stakeholder permissions across life-cycle phases, ensuring compliance with organizational policies encoded in formal models.
3. **Authentication Systems**: Semantic drift mitigation (e.g., via declarative life-cycle languages like Kamburjan et al.’s [50] approach) may require multi-factor authentication (MFA) for DT runtime instances to prevent unauthorized modifications to physical-physical model alignment.
4. **Fail-Safe Mechanisms**: Safety-critical systems in AECO DTs (e.g., performance analysis of LEED Platinum buildings) would benefit from **fail-safe validation** protocols tied to BIM/IoT data integrity, leveraging automated normalization (as in Gerber et al.’s [33] study) to detect and correct inconsistencies in real-time.
5. **Risk Assessment Approaches**: The emphasis on **semantic coherence** (e.g., Abbasi et al.’s [49] work) suggests integration with risk frameworks like FAIR (Factor, Action, Impact, Remedy) to quantify and prioritize vulnerabilities in DT lifecycles, particularly in complex AECO ecosystems where manual effort is minimized via MDE.
6. **Ontological Frameworks**: Oakes et al.’s [51] advancements in ontological frameworks for service-oriented DTs imply the use of **OWL/DL** or **RDFS** for semantic interoperability, which inherently supports safety by enforcing standardized vocabularies for threat detection (e.g., identifying malicious data drift patterns).

**Technologies/Methods Mentioned in Context (Indirectly Relevant)**:
- **BIM Integration**: Proprietary tools like Bentley’s iTwin.js enable real-time data synchronization but require explicit security audits to mitigate risks in proprietary ecosystems.
- **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)**: Enables governance via formalized policies (e.g., architectural decisions encoded in metamodels), indirectly supporting safety by reducing cognitive overload and ensuring consistency across stakeholders.
- **IoT Platforms**: Critical for real-time data acquisition (e.g., Opoku et al.’s [34] library setting) but demand robust security layers to protect against data tampering or injection attacks.

**Critical Gap**: While the documents highlight challenges in **data integration** and **stakeholder coordination**, they do not specify explicit security architectures (e.g., encryption standards, audit trails, or compliance certifications). The absence of MDE-based security frameworks (e.g., automated threat modeling) or privacy-preserving techniques (e.g., federated learning for DTs) suggests a need for future research in this domain.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS
block_3_docs_retrieved:
_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],
block_4_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
